title: Dog Limping Worsening with Exercise: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog limping more after activity? Learn why exercise-induced lameness happens, how urgent it is, and how to help your vet diagnose the issue.
content_html:
TL;DR: Limping that worsens with exercise usually indicates soft tissue or joint issues and requires a vet exam within 24-48 hours. Providing a video of your dog's gait at home is a vital tool to help your veterinarian accurately diagnose the source of pain.

What does it mean if my dog starts limping during or after exercise?

When a dog's limp becomes more pronounced during or after physical activity, it typically indicates that the mechanical stress of movement is aggravating an underlying issue. Unlike 'cold lameness' where a dog is stiff after sleep but improves as they warm up, exercise-induced lameness often points toward soft tissue injuries, such as ligament tears, muscle strains, or progressive joint conditions like osteoarthritis.

When is exercise-induced limping considered a veterinary emergency?

  • The urgency level for a limp that worsens with exercise is generally Medium, requiring an exam within 24 to 48 hours.
  • If your dog is still eating, drinking, and putting some weight on the leg, you can likely wait for a scheduled appointment.
  • Seek immediate care if the dog becomes completely non-weight-bearing.
  • Seek emergency care if there is significant heat and swelling in the limb or if your dog shows signs of extreme distress.

How can a video of my dog's limp help the veterinarian?

  • Dogs often hide pain at the clinic due to adrenaline, so a video of them in their home environment is a vital diagnostic tool.
  • Take a video of your dog walking at home specifically when the limp is most visible.
  • Record the gait from the side and from behind to allow the veterinarian to perform a more accurate visual triage.
  • Clear footage helps the vet identify which joint or muscle group is the likely source of the problem before the physical exam begins.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

A thorough lameness examination is critical for diagnosis, including evaluation at rest, rising, and during locomotion on flat or inclined surfaces to note single- or multiple-limb lameness, with severity related to activity. Observe for head elevation with forelimb lameness and head drop with hindlimb lameness during weight-bearing on the affected limb, and note any shortened stride. Limbs should be assessed from distal to proximal, palpating bones, joints, and soft tissues for swelling, pain, instability, crepitation, reduced range of motion, and muscle atrophy. Serial examinations before and after exercise may be necessary for subtle lameness. Imaging techniques, including radiography, ultrasonography, CT, and MRI, are helpful diagnostic procedures.

Chapter: Orthopedics, Immunology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1198)

faqs.question: Should I still take my dog for short walks?
faqs.answer: Until you have seen a veterinarian, it is best to strictly limit activity. Use a short leash for potty breaks only and avoid any running, jumping, or stairs.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog aspirin for the pain?
faqs.answer: No. Never administer human pain medications to dogs, as many are toxic. Some can also prevent your vet from being able to prescribe the correct canine-specific anti-inflammatories immediately.

faqs.question: What are common causes for this type of limp?
faqs.answer: Common causes include Cranial Cruciate Ligament (CCL) tears, tendonitis, or the early stages of arthritis that flares up with repetitive motion.
